free_text: Angular distributions of 137mCe and 137gCe from the
134Ba(alpha,n) reaction
Cross
section averages.
The Argonne National Laboratory 152 cm
cyclotron.
The irradiations had a duration of 3-4 h
at a current of approximately 0.2 microA. Before each
bombardment an alignment experiment was performed to
insure that the beam passed through the center of the
target and catcher foils. The catcher foil had a
diameter of 10 cm and was cut into 10 concentric
rings of equal width by a die and press. The angular
interval subtended by each ring was determined by the
target to catcher distance and the intervals were
usually in the range from 2-3 degrees. Cerium was
radiochemically separated from each ring.
NaI(Tl) detectors
The data were analyzed by means of a
least-squares computer program.
The error bars in the figures indicate the magnitude
of all random errors in the experiments. The errors
for the ground state product are much larger than for
the isomer because of the mode of decay.
Random errors at low energies typically
average about 10%. At the higher energies the
uncertainty in the results is somewhat larger than 10%.
There is an error of less than 5% in the
gravimetric chemical yields.
Compiler comment: According to the ENSDF database
137Ce ground state T1/2=9.0 HR and isomeric state
T1/2=34.4 HR. Authors give it the other way according
to the assignment of 1970.
